title: Money over patients every time
pros: The actual employees and leadership in the hospital are the best reason to stay. Great teamwork and they give the best care.
cons: Corporate rules, productivity tracking, supply purchases, and staffing expectations show that the last thing they care about is the welfare of their patients. The most important thing is giving the corporate leaders more money in their pockets. We are told we have to do more and more tasks with less resources. We are told we have to follow certain charting guidelines , go to classes, and fill out forms because evidence based research by our professional nursing specialty groups shows it’s important. But if we try to use the recommendations of those same groups for safe staffing ratios we get told their research isn’t good enough. Raises are almost non existent. My coworkers and the desire to take care of the people in my community are what keep me here, but it is getting harder and harder to stay with a company that shows they just don’t care. A CEO that makes 24 million should be able to give a little more to his hospitals.

title: Bait and Switch Hiring practices
pros: It is a place to work
cons: I feel it’s important to warn others about the unethical and immoral practices of this company, especially recruiter R.Reyna. I applied, interviewed and negotiated an offer for a Dayshift position, only to discover after completing the weeks long process that the shift on my offer letter was for nightshift. At no point did I ever made it seem like I was opened for nightshift, all of my communication with this recruiter only made mention of dayshift, even through emails I only wrote about the dayshift position; however, only after I had declined another offer had I been made aware that the dayshift position I was so looking forward, had already been filled weeks before I even applied for it (why was it still up on the website then ??) This kind of bait-and-switch tactic not only wastes time but can have a significant impact on nurses who depend on clear and honest communication when making career decisions. It’s deeply concerning to think how many other professionals may have been similarly misled. Recruiters have a responsibility to act with integrity, especially in industries like nursing, where our livelihoods and patient care are at stake. I hope this review encourages others to approach this agency with caution and holds them accountable for their actions. Transparency and professionalism should be the standard—not the exception.

They keep staffing at a bare minimum. They expect RN to break techs, they don’t have any break relief, they use managers to break nurses and they don’t want to take care of the pts needs. They cut staff as soon as there are discharges, even 1 hour before the end of the shift. When you have had 9 high acuity pts all day and they start sending nurse home, it’s defeating. I have tried all manners of addressing the issue. They don’t care. It’s all about numbers and profit. The pts are violent and they still won’t staff by acuity. Someone will be severely injured and /or killed by a violent, methamphetamine high pt someday. I have seen the worst assaults on staff regularly. Tenet just doesn’t care about the staff or pts.

title: Great if you LOVE headaches! 😃
pros: Trainers do their best to get you certified. Able to bring children on route (3 & Up)
cons: Very disorganized establishment. No communication or consideration as a whole. Regardless of hourly wage, you'd rarely see your money due to taxes and medical benefits(which by the way they pressure new hires to register for yet it isn't worth the trouble/pay decrease), and it'll be FOREVER before they remove them. During training you'll only get paid $10 hr and they'll let taxes/benefits leave you with $0...I recieved 3 checks of $0! Personally, they've been a setback financially since I started. Only great quality about them are their trainers. STA hides behind the mask of being family oriented when in reality all they truly care about is routes. (Which they'll snatch from you without prior notice and lie and say that district did it) We've ran out of gas twice and all they could tell us was to find a bus that had enough to run the route. I have SO MANY disappointments with being here when I initially had high hopes of this being where I'd retire from. If you choose or are begged to do any extra routes or field trips be sure to take a photo of all hours/miles bc they'll "lose your paperwork" in a heartbeat or promise to give extra hours and it'll be missing come payday. There's a rivalry between dispatch and safety and it displayed over the radio and in the office everyday. It's pretty much ran like a independent start-up instead of a reputable transportation company.

title: School Bus Driving Is A Job, Not A Career
pros: (*) Rewarding if you want to help keep school bus student transportation available to get students to & from brick & mortar schools. (*) Currently still free training for those who don't have a CDL &/or S license endorsements to qualify to drive a bus. (*) With the nationwide shortage of qualified school bus drivers, if you are a diligent, safe driver, committed to the mission, if you don't like the current bus provider, many of the competition are offering reasonable to exorbitant bonuses to get you to change companies. The question you need to ask yourself is are you doing for the community you live in or support nearby, for better working conditions & better maintained equipment, or strictly the money. (*) If you have time & desire, there is typically additional work for sports & school outings, if you don't mind obligating additional time to fill certain trips during the day (limited), & many evenings &/or weekends. (*) If you are bored at home, want to be active (operational engagement) in retirement, or need some supplemental income for those who may be financially challenged at the moment, & your health, driving record, background checks, & ability to get the specialized licenses, this just might be a potential opportunity to explore. (*) Just like many other jobs, or jobs under the guise of careers, there are many challenges if you are up to the challenges. You have to love working with kids, their parents, the school staff, & operations, including many temperamental mechanics, which are relied upon severely to allow you to perform your job. (*) Typically working 2.25-3 hrs in the morning (approx. 6am to 9am), & then returning for 2.25-3 hours in the afternoon (approx. 1:45 to 4-4:30pm)
cons: (*) Not a career. Careers should be considered as full-time financially self-sustaining occupations, whereas school bus driving is more like a part-time job or paid volunteer with somewhat lower pay, in hopes of finding enough qualified drivers, to help students in the community get to/from school. (*) Unless you are working 35-40 or 50 hours a week in a profession with real benefits, reality is that it is truly a part-time or seasonal support job. (*) Benefits may be referenced, but the realities are that they are limited in scope, & typically require 30 hours min per week to obtain, so majority of employees don't qualify. (*) On school off days, or extended breaks, you typically don't get paid from sub-contracted bus providers, unless a union, or school district contracts require otherwise. (*) In the same scenario, in today's health emergencies, like COVID-19, or snow/excessive cold declared remote learning days, you lose non-guaranteed income. (*) Regardless of drivers finding unpaid days off, even though you previously committed yourself, & were scheduled, but school transportation was cancelled, the office personnel above the drivers still get paid, but not you as a driver. (*) Unless you take extra trips, (which are both bid separately, or given out without bid based upon seniority), separate from Home To School, & School To Home, you must be willing to tie up (9-10 months & avail. for maybe 4.5-6 hours, spread out over 10-11 hours day) of your lives each school day, for a max of 180 days of potential work, if you work the full school year.

title: Not one of the places to work
pros: Atlantic Heath used to be a great place to work, but if I’m being honest it gets worse and worse every year. My last day was the most empowering day of my life. I love the fact that i never have to go back to that god forsaken place ever again.
cons: Atlantic Health protects management at all costs. The company values are extremely toxic. The “PRIDE” values are fake. Everything is all about money, not about the patients. They will try to get rid of you for speaking up for what is right. Doing incomplete investigations about anything brought to management, and then when they don’t find what you said was the case they use what you said against you and label anything you say going forward “unsubstantiated.” “Drinking the koolaid” is extremely encouraged. Atlantic Health has gone so far down the wrong path (specifically in the last 2 years) AHS used to be a great place fo work, morale was great, people were happy… but everything changed. The benefits are awful now - even WITHIN facility. No perks, even BIG huge celebration events they always find a way to take the cheap way out. For example: Fortune 100 best companies to work for for the (however many) year in a row heres a donut. They had us pay for our own $5 meal at a celebration picnic? After we just worked in the trenches through covid caring for sick patients not sure if going in that room caring for that patient would be our last time… thats a slap in the face and a joke. The company has been know to force the people who have been working in the company since way back in the day when they used to give out pensions, to resign so they are unable fo collect their pension. AHS is a horrible company to work for, they do not care about their employees at all.

title: Another Mass Lay Off
pros: Somewhat decent pay for associates, but does it matter when your job isn't secure??
cons: Another disappointing decision from corporate: eliminating all nurses in urgent care, regardless of tenure or performance. This feels like another example of leadership prioritizing small cost savings and lining their own pockets over patient safety and quality of care. Nurses were informed through a last minute Teams meeting where the director read from a prepared script about pre- and post- COVID staffing and the decision to return to so-called “national staffing standards.” No questions were allowed. Meanwhile, 16 job postings for medical assistants have already appeared in order to replace these asaociates (some nurses have already had the unknowing pleasure of training their MA replacements and learned that fact during the meeting! Wonderful!). If the change truly reflects national standards, even minimal research quickly raises questions about that explanation. It’s incredibly disheartening to see decisions like this being made at the expense of the nurses who have consistently supported these clinics and truly care about the patients they serve. Don't visit their urgent cares if your goal is to receive safe, competent care.

title: Medically broke
pros: I make decent money. But I lose it in my benefit "package". Between health insurance, dental insurance, vision, etc, well over $1000 a month.
cons: Anyone that has ever worked for a factory or public entity will understand how horrendous our health insurance is. For a medical company, it's pathetic. I pay well over $500 a month, have a $3000 deductible and pay a great deal out of pocket for just about everything. So basically, as a generally healthy person, I pay roughly $6000 a year with an over $3000 deductable. In other words, since I literally never spend $9000 a year on health issues, I am spending a ridiculous amount for something I never use. There are no "freebies" at all for employees in regards to illness. Only prevention. For instance, I worked at Mansfield prior to the Ohiohealth takeover and if you visited the ER and had to be admitted, your admission was covered 100%. I get that healthcare is changing, but our insurance is pathetic compared to non health care entities. Talk to any employee of a major factory or a public city/state job and you will understand the difference. Ohiohealth really should be embarrassed by their health insurance package. I'm not clear why employees think the leave package and vacation time is "amazing". Earning 1 day a month as a full time employee is hardly "amazing" or nearly comparable to many other companies.

title: Stay away from Rose schools!
pros: Great students, no work to take home, open space forces you to collaborate with colleagues and have open communication. School staff is generally supportive and positive.
cons: Benefits more complex than the script of Inception, upper management is repressive, condescending and seems to be bored out of their mind so they have teachers fill endless excel sheets that get discarded the following quarter. The company is completely dissociated with the actual goal and population they serve, they use old and irrelevant pedagogical methods, Kansas University methodologies are the only way to go for them and they refuse to look elsewhere. The salaries are low and every year you get a little presentation where they make you believe they are the highest paying charter school in town, this is a lie. Teachers with graduate degrees are hired under 40K! There's also no real fall or spring break, the last two quarters are extremely difficult and full of anxiety. I observed a lot of racial and ethnic biases from upper management that I found appalling. If your name is somewhat unusual they will not make any effort to pronounce it correctly -even after being corrected- and that says a lot about a company and their inclusivity mindset. Everyone is son-of, nepotism is abhorrent especially when teachers with post-graduate degrees and year of experience are treated like incompetent children. And finally, this is not a real teaching job, they will say they are not a credit mill, but that is what they truly are. Which is fine, credit mills can get all kinds of students out of trouble, but let's call a spade a spade.

title: 2026 - Teacher that worked for the district
pros: Soaring Eagles is best school, I never felt more supportive then here. Teachers can have a click-vibe to them, but tend to be friendly enough to get things done. You can make an impact with students coming from troubling parentless homes. If I wasn't moving, due to rising prices and pay rates, I would have requested to stay at this school.
cons: Career Reediness Academy, I started at this school and came to need legal support from an outside teachers union. As a person with a Principal Degree, I didn't try to overstep what was happening, it just that I had no support from the primary school principal. Students walked out randomly, cursing any adult out, then found them coming back to class with no consequences. Half the time you can find them smoking marijuana on the bridge outside of the Pamela Anderson elementary. Out of the four Primary subjects - The English Teacher left in October, Math in December, and History was a full time sub till January. We connected with students, made them work hard and tried to be sure that they received a quality education. You will not find a supportive admin team, principal is set in his ways. Yet you will come to like the instructional Coach, she has helped extremely with classroom curriculum. I hope she finds a better school site. If they punish her in some way from this review, it speaks volumes to what has happened with the recover program. Soaring Eagles - As said, teachers can have clicks, but that's normal in education.

title: It's teaching... probably best you will get in this profession
pros: Depends on where you work. Admin can be a pain or they can be hands off. Pray for the latter.
cons: Steps are not guaranteed and you will never get them back if the county decides not to give one. For instance, in 15 years, my pay has been frozen SIX TIMES. So if the county feels like there's not enough money in the budget, the FIRST THING they always do is not give teachers a raise (the school board decides this) so you dont get your step increase. So if you dont get your step for step 11 lets say, the following year you are still at 11. The year after you hope you get your step to 12. They dont step you up to 13. I have federal government friends who have their pay frozen for 6 months and they all freak out. When it's unfrozen, they all get back pay. That doesnt happen in teaching. So in 15 years I have lost about 20K in raises which also effects my retirement. I will get less in retirement.

title: Smoke and Mirrors - Surrounding Virginia districts (NOT Loudoun) may be better options in the long term
pros: *Competitive teacher salaries for teaching in the area *Adequate office supplies provided to teachers *Diverse student population of amazing students whose families are from around the world and nation- This dynamic kept me in FCPS longer than I should have stayed for my own wellness. *If you are enjoy "playing the game" and work politics, you may do very well.
cons: *Excessive and exploitive workload, increasing every year despite promises from district leaders that they are "working on it". My workload has dramatically increased every year and have been beyond sustainable levels for nearly a decade in this district. The last seven years have been eye opening. I finally have accepted it's just going to keep getting worse. *Poor roll out of district policies that impact classroom teachers *Leadership run schools like dictators. Each school is its own world, with little apparent oversight, and classroom teachers have little to no input. Your experience will depend solely on the strength of your admin. Do not expect any support from the district. The "customer" is always right and so is admin, even when either group is clearly in the wrong. I've heard of countless lawsuits against admin from teachers, in which the teacher won or was awarded money for their case. The cases I've heard of have involved harassment, endangerment, or prejudice (ageism is super common here). Meanwhile, the administrators involved are never reprimanded or fired. I've known people who worked in FCPS food services and custodial departments and they report the same experiences, if not worse. *They've had four superintendents since I've worked in this district. All but one (she quit) have been PR/spin doctor hires. Things have gotten even worse in the last year while the district messaging to the community is "everything's great!". FCPS sounds and looks great on paper. I was won over by their mission statement and supposed policies. They do not measure up to their own talk. *Health benefits and pension is not as good as it used to be, which make the pay rate worth even less especially living in a high cost of living area. Any pay raise ends up actually being a pay cut with good PR because it does not keep up with the incredible rising costs of living in Northern Virginia. *Despite what they say or how much you do, all non-leadership staff are easily discarded. *If you are a popular and good teacher, you will be overloaded with the most sensitive caseloads and difficult situations every single year. If you ask for fairness in how those caseloads are distributed between grade level or subject matter teams, you will be ignored at best and punished in some way at worst. My school lost nearly a teacher per grade level for this reason. One grade level is losing more than one of their outstanding teachers for this reason. Admin will do anything to avoid issues that come to their office but do not concern themselves with the impact this has on their staff. The burnout from being exploited in this district is horrendous. I know people who teach in Falls Church, Fairfax City, Chantilly, Reston, and Centreville. It's the same story everywhere. *On that note, if you're good in the classroom, the admin I've worked with will do everything to keep you there and stifle/mislead you from any other opportunities you are interested in within the system. We've lost several teachers who were refused interviews for non-classroom teacher roles, of which they exceeded requirements, because admin told them they were "too good" to be "pulled" from the classroom. Increasingly, this district's leaders only supports the advancement of their personal friends or average-mediocre teachers. I've seen several teachers who were in legitimate trouble or had problematic behaviors be given amazing opportunities just to get them out o the classroom. I've seen their peers in tears in the hallway after school over what an insult it is to their own hard work and covering for those weak staff members. Do NOT do what it takes to get Teacher of the Year or be a highly requested teacher by parents. Admin will then never support you in changing roles. My last four admin (two schools) only think of the NOW and not the consequences of their choices. That is why my current school is losing so many outstanding teachers, often to other fields entirely.

title: The WORST place to start your career!!
pros: The facilities are nice and kids are cute.
cons: This was by far the most stressful, toxic environment that I've ever worked in. If you're a new teacher I'd STRONGLY advise you to teach somewhere else. This school will make you question if you truly want to teach. You will not have a life outside of work if you work here. It is no different than Success Academy. Zeta uses the same questionable practices. They don't take special education seriously at all and weed out the kids they call "low flyers" instead of giving them the support they need. Parents and admin can contact you at any time of day and you get reprimanded if you don't respond. Zeta will mold you into being the teacher THEY want you to be, not the kind of teacher YOU want to be. Zeta wants teachers who humiliate students who don't perform well and scare kids into doing the right thing. The school only cares about data and public perception and truthfully your mental health and the kids' mental health don't matter. Your admin will send you passive-aggressive messages if 2 out of the 35 kids in your class aren't paying attention and ultimately fire you for any reason they want. You will spend your weekends completing all of the tedious work your kids are doing that week so you can be "intellectually prepared". They will send you a deliverable at 4 pm and demand that it be completed by 8z Everyone that works here is in it for themselves. Zeta fosters community at the beginning of the year but the competition for the best data leads to a cutthroat environment. Everyone gossips about each other and there is so much drama going on at all times. This environment is also due to the admin favoring teachers with high data and bullying those who do not have it. Every negative review you read is true.. take the positive ones written by Admin with a grain of salt. My mental health improved dramatically when I left this school. YOU DESERVE BETTER THAN ZETA.

title: A Demanding Environment That Builds Teaching Skills
pros: If you can handle a rigorous and highly prescribed instructional model, there is real opportunity for growth as an educator. The structure can strengthen classroom management, planning, and instructional clarity especially for newer teachers who want strong systems. Williamsburg Middle School, in particular, was a genuinely positive environment. The leadership team there was solid, supportive, and grounded. The educators and leaders at the school level clearly care about students and about each other, which made day to day work meaningful.
cons: At the network level, Uncommon is a massive charter organization, and with that scale comes familiar issues. Like many large inner-city charter networks rooted in “achievement gap” narratives, there are elements of meta-racism embedded in the system often tied to white saviorism and top down decision making. That said, it’s important to name the distinction: while the network structure carries these problems, many of the educators and school based leaders actively push back against them. The people working directly with students often lead with care, reflection, and genuine commitment, even when the broader system falls short.

title: Too Much For Any Teacher To Handle
pros: - Working with great students - Alleviating the teacher shortage in the country
cons: - You get hazed into being an employee at all Uncommon Schools. Within my first month at Uncommon, I received hundreds - yes, hundreds - of emails from leadership members with feedback under the guise of being "solutions oriented" and "positive." In reality, they are invitations for 6AM "practice clinics" in which you do rote memorization of the script that Uncommon Schools uses to teach people how they want you to engage with students. This place goes beyond the expectations that most people have when they enter teaching: of course everyone is aware of the staff shortages, tight budgets, and needing to be conscious of your work-life balance. Uncommon Schools does not care about your work life balance, about your salary, and about being culturally responsive. That's just their tactic for having good advertising material. - You will not build relationships with your students if you follow the Uncommon Schools script through and through. It is punitive, mean, and actively disregards students' IEPs, 504s, and cultural backgrounds. You will send students out of the classroom more times than you invite them in. And your will-power or dedication to "do the right thing" will not matter here: I regularly tried to reason with my coach and principal that it wasn't necessary to always send students out of the classroom, especially students whose IEPs required that they receive multiple redirections for being off task or confused. These conversations regularly ended in them concluding that I "just wasn't sending students out enough." To follow up on this, multiple members of leadership would randomly walk into my classroom and send students out on their own volition if they thought I wasn't doing it enough or as rigorously. No matter how hard I tried to be in charge of my own classroom and build trust with students, it never felt like enough because at any moment in the day, someone from leadership would walk in and just make students and myself feel unsafe. - Leadership members have gossiped about me in the past and I have walked in on them doing so. Fellow teachers have listened in on my personal conversations in the past and reported them to leadership to get me into trouble. There is a horrible work culture here and if you are not "aligned" with backstabbing your colleagues, you will be singled out and ostracized. - To add onto the other comments about long work hours: I know that for many teachers, this is expected and doesn't come as much of a surprise. The reason it is worse at Uncommon is because the school days are longer already (I worked from 7AM - 4 PM), but they will also invite you to Practice Clinics at 6AM, schedule IEP meetings for 5PM, and send you on overnight school trips with no added stipend or compensation. - In my first year, I did not make my salary. Every first year teacher is given a contract with a stated salary, but in fine writing, they prohibit you from making your stated salary and cut you short thousands of dollars. If you choose this employer, read your contract carefully, and then follow up to confirm the actual amount of money that you will be making, not the number they put on your contract. - Do your research about Black at Uncommon, the history behind Uncommon Schools' militaristic teaching style, and their low teacher retention rates. There is a huge disconnect between the positive reviews that current Uncommon employees are posting here on Glassdoor and the actual data about how many people can withstand working even a year at this organization without becoming brainwashed.

title: What they won’t tell you in the interview.

cons: - The educational director is extremely unprofessional in how she describes the children in the program, which is troubling. - Classroom observations yield generic feedback that provides little actual value to you as a teacher. - The turnover rate is high…unbelievably high. This is likely due to the fact that “management” consists of useless PD’s and micromanagement on all fronts, while providing little ~actual~ support in the classroom where it matters. - Kiss your lunchtime goodbye. You get 20 minutes to scarf down food as quickly as possible before the next class is unleashed on you. title: Just look away, look away, look away...
pros: Teachers genuinely LOVE their job, and you can see it! The positive "vibes" definitely rubs off, but the cons outweighs any benefits...
cons: This job is demanding, and requires way too much work outside of contract hours. Weekly lesson plans are required to be submitted. I was never required by prior districts to do this so this is still odd to me. Payroll did not enroll me into the NJ Pension Funds until January which is the middle of the school year, and therefore, my paycheck was slashed drastically in January because they were taking out what I owed for the six months I wasn't enrolled, and on top of that the premium for the current month. In other words, about $500-$600 were taken off each paycheck for three months which as a person who lives paycheck to paycheck isn't quite ideal!!! I should have been enrolled earlier, and when they enrolled me back in October, I should have at least gotten a WARNING in October, and I could of saved money from October to December to even myself out from January to March but no one warned me!!! This has never happened to me before in any other district. Extended School Day (7:15AM-4PM). Extended School Year (August-June). No REAL Salary Schedule. I was verbally told roughly some numbers during the interview, but I wouldn't know how much I'll be earning with a +30 if I decide to get my +30. I would rather want a salary schedule. Teacher contracts are 10 months, meaning no pay in the Summer. Students were underserviced (ESL & SPED) in prior years making students a bit more needy, and more explicit teaching is required. TOTALLY SCREWED UP THE SUMMER PAY for SUMMER SCHOOL. They basically kept saying "Sorry, Sorry, Sorry", but no money was seen until almost the end of Summer School after I sent a nasty email, and magically, money came through, but I had to pick it up at the physical school during the Summer, when I was working virtually. Then they promised Teachers a "gift card" to pretty much say "sorry" some more as a "bonus", and never contacted me personally about the gift card, knowing damn well I needed money, and then it made it seem as if it was my fault that I didn't pick up my "gift card"?! Heres, another story...In January of 2022, I needed a work verification form because I went to another state, and contacted this school again. LONG BEHOLD, 4 months later in April, I realize that they never sent the form because my current state verified they never received anything from this school! I contacted them again, and all they said was "Sorry for the delays" like are you kidding me? 4 months later, and I still don't got the work verification form from this school. Other remarks that makes this place even worse: I asked for more money before I left the school at the end of the school year, and even gave them a position proposal where I obviously would qualify for the job position I proposed since I wanted more pay. It was never discussed, and in fact, actually ignored, and after I quit, I found out they created a similar position I PROPOSED to them, and gave it to someone else. How nice huh? GLAD I LEFT! I was only being used.

We have a team of professional leaders who are visible and available in each building. Coaching has also been a new feature to College Achieve that has benefited all of our teachers. Weekly professional development has improved with feedback from teachers, walkthroughs, state visits, and interim assessments as we use this data to drive our instruction. Each building social worker and dean works collectively to establish a safe and positive learning environment. Our new administration, coaches, and teaching staff are extremely hard working and invested in their students' abilities.

title: Teacher

cons: Lots of FALSE promises regarding bonuses or incentives. They entice you to do things to get " your money" (quoted by principal), however you do the work and then they never follow through.... happened more than once this year. End of year bonus. Well, if the principal doesn't low ball you for her "professional observations" which for most employees who she feels she doesn't like because they spoke up and challenged her "system" she will lower your score (Whistleblower law broken here by employer). They make you do an end of year presentation (more work on top of what you have already done). And if, IF, they feel that you are worthy of the bonus, then you will get the bonus pay the FOLLOWING YEAR in increments. So those that were not asked to return or chose not to return, even though they did the work and improved the gap, will not get acknowledged. In fact, it was observed that 3 teachers did lower the educational gap the previous year with the PROOF through the PARCC testing results. However, because they weren't the "favorites" of the principal, they DID NOT receive any bonus compensation. Even though there was PROOF from the state testing that they did their job and then some.

title: Good first job with steady hours and supportive team
pros: Walmart is a solid place to gain retail experience. The job teaches you a lot about customer service, inventory, and working in a fast-paced environment. Management at my store was generally supportive and flexible with scheduling, which made it easy to balance work and personal life. The team atmosphere was friendly and everyone helped each other out when the store got busy.
cons: The work can be repetitive, especially during slower hours, and dealing with upset customers can be stressful at times. Pay is fair for entry-level retail, but it doesn’t go up quickly unless you move into a higher role. Weekend and holiday shifts are almost always required.

Steady employment with consistent hours available in many locations. Employee discounts on eligible merchandise and access to additional savings programs.
cons: Heavy workloads, especially during holidays and peak shopping periods. Physically demanding work that often involves standing for long periods, lifting, and repetitive tasks. Inconsistent or changing schedules that can make work-life balance difficult. Understaffing during busy shifts, leading to increased stress and responsibilities.

title: Woman on woman discrimination: upper management approved 👍🤡
pros: The work itself was simple and I enjoyed working in my department.
cons: There was a woman in another department (lower management/department manager of a department I did not work in) that frequently would neglect her duties to “make sure” I was completing mine. On multiple occasions when I was working, and on-task, I was asked what I was doing, what I was supposed to be doing, and even reported to upper management because she believed I was bringing a non-employee to our break room to “flirt”. I in fact was helping another employee find another employee to secure a ride home. The employee I was assisting was female with a male presenting haircut. They continued to refer to her with he/him pronouns and implied I was being promiscuous. The upper management watched the tapes back and actually wrote me up for the false offense. I later quit when I found a new job with far better pay AND benefits.
